era_context: The Achaemenid Persian Empire is consolidating its conquest of Egypt (completed in 525 BCE). In Greece, the Archaic period continues; Athens remains under Peisistratid tyranny. In India, the Buddha is in his mid-teaching phase. China is in the Spring and Autumn period. The Mediterranean world is experiencing increased trade and cultural exchange.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (limited written records for this specific year)

events.title: Cambyses II's campaigns in Nubia and Libya
events.description: Following the conquest of Egypt, Cambyses II launches military campaigns into Nubia (Kush) and Libya to consolidate Persian control over North Africa. These campaigns are less well-documented than the Egyptian conquest but represent the extension of Achaemenid imperial authority southward and westward. The campaigns are described in later sources as partially successful, though the extent of Persian control over these regions remains debated.
events.certainty_note: Herodotus describes these campaigns, but the dating and extent are uncertain. The campaigns likely occurred in the years immediately following the Egyptian conquest (525 BCE), but the specific year 526 BCE is approximate.
cross_references: Follows from the conquest of Egypt in 525 BCE

events.title: Continued development of Greek Archaic pottery and sculpture
events.description: Greek artisans continue to develop the Archaic style in pottery, sculpture, and architecture. This period sees the refinement of black-figure pottery technique and the production of monumental stone sculpture. The Archaic period (c. 800–480 BCE) is characterized by increasing naturalism, the development of the Greek alphabet, and the emergence of the polis as the dominant political unit.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence: pottery, sculpture, architectural remains

events.certainty_note: The development of Greek Archaic art is well-documented through archaeological evidence. The specific year 526 BCE is part of a broader cultural continuum; no specific event is dated to this year.
cross_references: Part of the broader Archaic period in Greece

disconfirming_evidence: Herodotus's account of Cambyses' Nubian campaign includes the story of an army being swallowed by a sandstorm, which modern scholars regard as legendary rather than historical. The extent of Persian control over Nubia and Libya is debated; some scholars argue that Persian authority was limited to the Nile Delta and did not extend significantly into Nubia or Libya. The dating of these campaigns to 526 BCE specifically is not well-supported by contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: The year 526 BCE is poorly documented overall. Greek sources are retrospective and focus on the Mediterranean. Persian sources are limited to later compilations. Egyptian sources provide administrative records but little narrative detail. Indian sources (Buddhist texts) are not specific to this year. The historical record for this year is thin, and much of what is attributed to it is either approximate dating or part of broader processes (cultural development, imperial consolidation) rather than discrete events.

graph_edges.note: Nubian and Libyan campaigns follow from the conquest of Egypt
